title: Comparing Video Models
concept: Different video architectures trade off speed, accuracy, and computational requirements depending on the application.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the core mechanism of I3D?

options.text: Inflating 2D network layers and weights into 3D
Running two parallel 2D streams on a CPU
Compressing video files into smaller formats

exercises.explain: Yes! I3D inflates successful 2D models into 3D to reuse learned image weights.

cards.title: Choosing the right tool
cards.body: If you are building an AI application to label soccer highlights, should you use C3D, a Two-Stream network, or I3D? There is no single best model. Each has massive tradeoffs in speed, accuracy, and ease of training.

cards.title: Model Tradeoffs
cards.body:
• C3D: Simple uniform baseline, but parameter-heavy and hard to train from scratch.
• Two-Stream: Strong accuracy by separating static detail from optical flow, but requires expensive flow pre-computation.
• I3D: State-of-the-art accuracy by reusing 2D pre-trained weights, but requires high GPU memory.

cards.title: Bikes vs. Trucks
cards.body: C3D is like a sturdy heavy truck: reliable but massive. Two-Stream is like a bicycle pulling a cart: fast, but you have to manually load the optical flow cart first. I3D is like a modern sports car: ultra-high performance but expensive to run.

cards.title: The optical flow bottleneck
cards.body: If a Two-Stream network needs to run in real-time, calculating dense optical flow (Farneback) on the fly becomes a major bottleneck. For edge devices, engineers might choose a lightweight 3D CNN instead.

cards.title: Optical flow is not dead
cards.body: You might think 3D CNNs make optical flow obsolete. In fact, combining them yields the best results! The top-performing I3D models actually run as a Two-Stream network, with one 3D CNN for RGB frames and another 3D CNN for optical flow!

cards.title: Video Model Scorecard
cards.body: Comparing the performance profile of major video models:

left.text:
Accuracy: High
Computation: High (requires pre-flow)
Transfer Learning: Yes (2D CNNs)

right.text:
Accuracy: Very High
Computation: High (3D activations)
Transfer Learning: Yes (Inflated 2D)

exercises.prompt: What is a major bottleneck when deploying Two-Stream networks in real-time?

options.text: Computing optical flow on the fly
The size of the output class labels
Converting the video to black and white

exercises.explain: Correct! Calculating dense optical flow at high frame rates requires massive processing power.

exercises.prompt: True or false: The best performing video classifiers often combine 3D CNNs with Two-Stream setups.
exercises.explain: True. Running an inflated 3D CNN on raw frames and another on optical flow captures features robustly.

exercises.sentence: While C3D is trained from scratch, I3D improves accuracy by bootstrapping weights from ___.
exercises.explain: I3D leverages 2D pre-trained weights from ImageNet to bootstrap spatiotemporal representations.

exercises.prompt: Which model is the most parameter-heavy and computationally expensive to train from scratch?
exercises.explain: C3D uses massive 3D convolutional layers and fully connected layers, making it highly resource-intensive.

exercises.prompt: You are building a real-time gesture recognition model for a smart home device with a tiny processor. Which design choice is best?

options.text: A lightweight 3D CNN with spatial downsampling and no optical flow calculation.
A full double-stream I3D model with dense Farneback optical flow.
An image classification network running at 1000 FPS.
None, smart home devices cannot run computer vision.

exercises.explain: Exactly! Edge devices have limited compute. Calculating optical flow is too heavy. A lightweight, optimized 3D CNN is the most sensible choice. Next: how do we detect and track individual moving objects? Next up: Video Object Detection and Tracking. Phase 8: Modern Object Detection is complete. Next up: Image Segmentation.
